_meta.description: Common smart contract vulnerability patterns for bug hunting

patterns.name: Reentrancy
patterns.description: External call made before state update, allowing attacker to re-enter the function
patterns.how_to_find: Look for external calls (transfer, call, send) BEFORE state changes (balance updates)
patterns.example_vulnerable: function withdraw() { token.transfer(msg.sender, balance); balances[msg.sender] = 0; }
patterns.example_fix: function withdraw() { uint amount = balances[msg.sender]; balances[msg.sender] = 0; token.transfer(msg.sender, amount); }

patterns.name: Flash Loan Attack
patterns.description: Price or state manipulation using large temporary borrowed funds within single transaction
patterns.how_to_find: Look for price calculations based on spot balances, LP token minting based on reserves
patterns.example_vulnerable: uint price = reserveA / reserveB; // can be manipulated with flash loan
patterns.example_fix: Use TWAP oracle or Chainlink price feeds

patterns.name: Oracle Manipulation
patterns.description: Reliance on manipulable price feeds or single-source oracles
patterns.how_to_find: Look for spot price usage, single oracle source, missing staleness checks
patterns.example_vulnerable: uint price = uniswapPair.getReserves(); // spot price, manipulable
patterns.example_fix: Use Chainlink with staleness check: require(block.timestamp - updatedAt < 3600)

patterns.name: Access Control Issues
patterns.description: Missing or improper access restrictions on privileged functions
patterns.how_to_find: Look for missing onlyOwner/onlyAdmin modifiers, public functions that should be restricted
patterns.example_vulnerable: function setPrice(uint _price) public { price = _price; }
patterns.example_fix: function setPrice(uint _price) public onlyOwner { price = _price; }

patterns.name: Integer Overflow/Underflow
patterns.description: Arithmetic operations that wrap around due to uint limits (pre-Solidity 0.8)
patterns.how_to_find: Look for unchecked arithmetic in Solidity <0.8, or explicit unchecked blocks
patterns.example_vulnerable: uint8 balance = 255; balance += 1; // wraps to 0
patterns.example_fix: Use Solidity 0.8+ or SafeMath library

patterns.name: Signature Replay
patterns.description: Signatures that can be reused across transactions, chains, or contracts
patterns.how_to_find: Look for missing nonce, chainId, or contract address in signed messages
patterns.example_vulnerable: bytes32 hash = keccak256(abi.encode(to, amount)); // missing nonce
patterns.example_fix: bytes32 hash = keccak256(abi.encode(to, amount, nonce++, block.chainid, address(this)));

patterns.name: Frontrunning / MEV
patterns.description: Transactions that can be profitably reordered or sandwich attacked
patterns.how_to_find: Look for slippage-sensitive swaps, missing deadline, predictable outcomes
patterns.example_vulnerable: swap(tokenA, tokenB, amountIn, 0); // minOut = 0, no slippage protection
patterns.example_fix: swap(tokenA, tokenB, amountIn, minAmountOut, deadline);

patterns.name: Unchecked Return Value
patterns.description: Return values from external calls not checked, assuming success
patterns.how_to_find: Look for transfer(), send(), low-level call() without return check
patterns.example_vulnerable: token.transfer(to, amount); // return value ignored
patterns.example_fix: require(token.transfer(to, amount), 'transfer failed'); // or use SafeERC20

patterns.name: Dangerous Delegatecall
patterns.description: Delegatecall to untrusted contract allowing storage manipulation
patterns.how_to_find: Look for delegatecall to user-controlled addresses or upgradeable proxies
patterns.example_vulnerable: target.delegatecall(data); // target is user-controlled
patterns.example_fix: Only delegatecall to trusted, immutable implementation contracts

patterns.name: Selfdestruct Issues
patterns.description: Force-sending ETH via selfdestruct or unprotected selfdestruct calls
patterns.how_to_find: Look for balance checks that can be bypassed, unprotected selfdestruct
patterns.example_vulnerable: require(address(this).balance == 0); // can be bypassed with selfdestruct
patterns.example_fix: Use accounting variables instead of address(this).balance

patterns.name: Timestamp Dependency
patterns.description: Reliance on block.timestamp for critical logic (can be manipulated by miners)
patterns.how_to_find: Look for block.timestamp in randomness, short time windows
patterns.example_vulnerable: uint random = uint(keccak256(abi.encode(block.timestamp)));
patterns.example_fix: Use Chainlink VRF for randomness, allow larger time windows

patterns.name: Denial of Service (Gas)
patterns.description: Unbounded loops or operations that can exceed block gas limit
patterns.how_to_find: Look for loops over dynamic arrays, batch operations without limits
patterns.example_vulnerable: for(uint i=0; i<users.length; i++) { pay(users[i]); }
patterns.example_fix: Implement pagination or pull-over-push pattern

patterns.name: Storage Collision
patterns.description: Storage slot collision between proxy and implementation contracts
patterns.how_to_find: Look for upgradeable proxies, check storage layout compatibility
patterns.example_vulnerable: Proxy slot 0: admin, Implementation slot 0: owner // collision!
patterns.example_fix: Use EIP-1967 storage slots, unstructured storage pattern

patterns.name: Rounding Errors
patterns.description: Precision loss in division operations, especially with tokens
patterns.how_to_find: Look for division before multiplication, small amount edge cases
patterns.example_vulnerable: uint share = (amount / totalSupply) * price; // precision loss
patterns.example_fix: uint share = (amount * price) / totalSupply; // multiply first

patterns.name: First Depositor Attack
patterns.description: First depositor can manipulate share price in vault-style contracts
patterns.how_to_find: Look for vault mint logic when totalSupply is 0 or very low
patterns.example_vulnerable: shares = (amount * totalSupply) / totalAssets; // 0 division risk
patterns.example_fix: Use virtual shares/assets offset, require minimum first deposit

patterns.name: Cross-Contract Reentrancy
patterns.description: Reentrancy through multiple contracts with shared state
patterns.how_to_find: Look for external calls between related contracts, shared storage
patterns.example_vulnerable: ContractA calls ContractB which calls back to ContractA
patterns.example_fix: Use global reentrancy lock across all related contracts

patterns.name: ERC20 Approve Race Condition
patterns.description: Double-spend vulnerability when changing allowance
patterns.how_to_find: Look for approve() calls that change non-zero allowance
patterns.example_vulnerable: token.approve(spender, newAmount); // race condition if oldAmount > 0
patterns.example_fix: Use increaseAllowance/decreaseAllowance or approve to 0 first

patterns.name: Unprotected Initialization
patterns.description: Initializer function can be called by anyone or called multiple times
patterns.how_to_find: Look for initialize() without initializer modifier or access control
patterns.example_vulnerable: function initialize(address _owner) public { owner = _owner; }
patterns.example_fix: function initialize(address _owner) public initializer { owner = _owner; }

patterns.name: Centralization Risk
patterns.description: Single admin/owner with excessive control over funds or protocol
patterns.how_to_find: Look for owner-only functions that can drain funds, pause, or change critical params
patterns.example_vulnerable: function emergencyWithdraw() onlyOwner { token.transfer(owner, balance); }
patterns.example_fix: Use timelock, multisig, or DAO governance for critical functions

patterns.name: Griefing Attack
patterns.description: Attacker can cause loss to others without direct profit
patterns.how_to_find: Look for operations that can be blocked or made expensive by others
patterns.example_vulnerable: Anyone can call liquidate() which may revert due to gas
patterns.example_fix: Design for adversarial conditions, use keeper incentives

patterns.name: Donation Attack
patterns.description: Direct token transfer (donation) manipulates internal accounting
patterns.how_to_find: Look for balance-based calculations that can be affected by donations
patterns.example_vulnerable: uint rate = address(this).balance / totalShares; // can donate ETH
patterns.example_fix: Use internal accounting variables, not actual balances

patterns.name: Bridge Vulnerability
patterns.description: Issues in cross-chain message passing, validation, or asset locking
patterns.how_to_find: Look for message validation, replay protection, asset lock/unlock logic
patterns.example_vulnerable: Missing chain ID validation in cross-chain messages
patterns.example_fix: Validate source chain, use nonces, verify message authenticity

patterns.name: Governance Attack
patterns.description: Flash loan or manipulation of governance voting
patterns.how_to_find: Look for vote weight based on current balance, missing snapshot
patterns.example_vulnerable: uint votes = token.balanceOf(voter); // flash loan attack
patterns.example_fix: Use vote snapshots, timelock on proposals

patterns.name: Unsafe Callback/Hook
patterns.description: Callbacks to untrusted contracts (ERC721/1155 hooks, flash loan callbacks)
patterns.how_to_find: Look for onERC721Received, onFlashLoan, or custom callbacks
patterns.example_vulnerable: IERC721(nft).safeTransferFrom(...); // triggers onERC721Received
patterns.example_fix: Be aware of reentrancy via callbacks, use checks-effects-interactions

patterns.name: Permit/Meta-Transaction Issues
patterns.description: Issues with EIP-2612 permit or meta-transactions
patterns.how_to_find: Look for permit() implementation, deadline checks, nonce handling
patterns.example_vulnerable: Missing deadline check, reusable signatures
patterns.example_fix: Validate deadline, use unique nonces, validate signer
